title: A Momentum-Regulated Model For Star Formation Efficiency in Giant Molecular Clouds
comment: Accepted for publication in Astronomy & Astrophysics (16 pages, 5 figures)
summary: We present a minimal analytic framework to investigate the star formation efficiency per free-fall time, $ε_{\rm ff}$, in giant molecular clouds (GMCs), focusing on the origin of the observed clustering around $ε_{\rm ff} \sim 0.01$. We model the time evolution of the turbulent velocity dispersion through a momentum balance between stellar feedback and turbulent dissipation, and show that this generically leads to a stable low-efficiency equilibrium with only weak dependence on global cloud properties. We extend the framework by including a phenomenological contribution from gravity-driven turbulence and find that both feedback- and gravity-driven motions converge to similar equilibrium states under typical GMC conditions. The efficiency can be expressed as the ratio between a gravitational velocity scale and an effective feedback velocity scale, providing a physically transparent interpretation of self-regulated star formation. The model provides a simple, physically motivated interpretation of observed gas-star formation scaling relations, including a Schmidt-like scaling at cloud scales and a Kennicutt-like scaling when averaged over cloud populations. Comparison with observed GMC properties shows agreement within a factor of a few and highlights the weak sensitivity of $ε_{\rm ff}$ to cloud parameters. Despite its simplicity, the framework captures the leading-order interplay between turbulence, gravity, and feedback, and provides a physically transparent explanation for the origin and robustness of low star formation efficiencies in GMCs.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a minimal analytic framework showing that star formation efficiency per free-fall time ($\epsilon_{\rm ff}$) in GMCs naturally converges to $\sim 0.01$ due to a balance between stellar feedback and turbulent dissipation, with weak dependence on global cloud properties. The efficiency is expressed as a ratio of gravitational to feedback velocity scales, providing a simple interpretation of self-regulated star formation and observed scaling relations.
AI.motivation: To explain the observed clustering of star formation efficiency per free-fall time ($\epsilon_{\rm ff} \sim 0.01$) in giant molecular clouds and understand why it shows such weak dependence on varying cloud properties.
AI.method: A minimal analytic framework modeling the time evolution of turbulent velocity dispersion via momentum balance between stellar feedback and turbulent dissipation, extended to include gravity-driven turbulence; efficiency expressed as ratio of gravitational to feedback velocity scales.
AI.result: The model shows that feedback-turbulence balance generically leads to a stable low-efficiency equilibrium ($\epsilon_{\rm ff} \sim 0.01$) with weak dependence on global cloud properties; both feedback- and gravity-driven turbulence converge to similar equilibrium states under typical GMC conditions. The framework reproduces Schmidt-like and Kennicutt-like scaling relations, with predictions matching observed GMC properties within a factor of a few.
AI.conclusion: Despite its simplicity, the analytic framework captures the leading-order interplay between turbulence, gravity, and feedback, providing a physically transparent explanation for the origin and robustness of low star formation efficiencies in GMCs.

title: Nitrogen rises to the top: evidence of enhanced mixing in very massive stars
comment: Accepted for publication in A&A letters
summary: Recent observations of young galaxies in the high-redshift Universe reveal signs of early enrichment of nitrogen. Extremely massive stars ($M \gtrsim 10^{2}-10^3\,M_\odot$) with strong stellar winds have been proposed as a potential driver of this phenomenon. Here, we show that the observed fraction of nitrogen rich stars with masses $\gtrsim 100M_\odot$ cannot be explained solely by mass loss, requiring significantly more efficient mixing beyond their convective cores than accounted in present evolutionary models. We compile a representative sample of 122 stars in the Tarantula Nebula of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) with masses $M \gtrsim 30\,M_\odot$. Nearly all stars with masses $M \gtrsim 100\,M_\odot$ exhibit strong nitrogen enrichment, by factors $\gtrsim 5-10$. We demonstrate that this trend cannot be reproduced by varying assumptions on binary fraction, star formation history, or mass-loss rates within ranges predicted by current empirical and theoretical models. In contrast, enhanced core overshooting of $α_\text{ov}\gtrsim 1$ can account for the observed enrichment, but leads to quasi-chemically homogeneous evolution that is inconsistent with the observed Hertzsprung-Russell diagram. While the origin of this discrepancy remains unclear, our results, in combination with observational and theoretical constraints on mass-loss rates, suggest the presence of efficient early mixing operating during or shortly after the formation of very massive stars. Such mixing models are currently not included in stellar evolution models. These findings have immediate implications for the formation, radial expansion, evolution, and final fates of stars at the upper mass end, and provide a potential pathway to explaining the rapid nitrogen enrichment observed in the high-redshift Universe.

AI.tldr: Observations of very massive stars (>100 M☉) in the LMC show strong nitrogen enrichment that cannot be explained by current models of mass loss, binary fraction, or star formation history, suggesting efficient early mixing beyond convective cores that is not accounted for in present stellar evolution models.
AI.motivation: To understand the observed early nitrogen enrichment in high-redshift galaxies, which has been attributed to extremely massive stars with strong stellar winds, by testing whether current stellar evolution models can reproduce the nitrogen enrichment fractions observed in a sample of very massive stars.
AI.method: Compiled a sample of 122 stars in the Tarantula Nebula (LMC) with masses ≥30 M☉, measured nitrogen enrichment factors, and tested whether varying assumptions on binary fraction, star formation history, mass-loss rates, and core overshooting within current empirical/theoretical ranges can reproduce the observed enrichment patterns.
AI.result: Nearly all stars with masses ≥100 M☉ show strong nitrogen enrichment (factors of 5-10), which cannot be reproduced by varying binary fraction, star formation history, or mass-loss rates. Enhanced core overshooting (α_ov≥1) can account for enrichment but leads to quasi-chemically homogeneous evolution inconsistent with the observed HR diagram.
AI.conclusion: Efficient early mixing operating during or shortly after the formation of very massive stars is required but not included in current models, with implications for the formation, evolution, and final fates of the most massive stars and a potential explanation for rapid nitrogen enrichment in the high-redshift Universe.

title: Kinetic Processes to Radio Burst: First Observational-driven Study in Coronal Loops
summary: Understanding the origin of coherent solar radio bursts requires linking macroscopic coronal structures with the kinetic processes responsible for wave generation. We investigate emissions from slowly positively drifting bursts (SPDBs), a specific type of solar radio emission. SPDBs provide observational constraints for modeling beam-plasma interactions in coronal loops, serving as a basis for a multiscale description. We employ a three--stage numerical framework that combines nonlinear force--free field (NLFFF) magnetic extrapolation, guiding--center simulations, and fully kinetic particle--in--cell (PIC) modeling. The background plasma density is described using a hydrostatic model consistent with active--region conditions, producing a plasma--frequency gradient comparable to that inferred from the observed SPDBs spectrum. Energetic electrons injected near the loop top evolve through magnetic mirroring, pitch--angle scattering, turbulence development, and partial precipitation. Evolved velocity distribution functions (EVDFs) are sampled after approximately one bounce period and used in PIC simulations to evaluate emission properties. The results show that the evolved beam distribution of energetic electrons predominantly excites beam--Langmuir waves and fundamental plasma emission along the loop, with the emission intensity gradually decreasing from the loop top toward the footpoint. The modest initial beam velocity of energetic electrons explains the inefficient generation of harmonic plasma emission. The temporal evolution of the modeled emission reproduces key SPDB characteristics, including the ~ 4s duration and frequency drift behavior. These results suggest plasma emission explains the mechanisms behind SPDB generation and demonstrate the feasibility of a unified model connecting coronal magnetic topology, particle transport, and radio emission.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a three-stage numerical framework (NLFFF extrapolation, guiding-center simulations, and PIC modeling) to study slowly positively drifting bursts (SPDBs) in solar radio emission. The results show that evolved beam distributions excite beam-Langmuir waves and fundamental plasma emission, with intensity decreasing from loop top to footpoint, reproducing key SPDB characteristics like ~4s duration and frequency drift.
AI.motivation: To understand the origin of coherent solar radio bursts by linking macroscopic coronal structures with kinetic processes responsible for wave generation, using SPDBs as observational constraints for modeling beam-plasma interactions in coronal loops.
AI.method: Three-stage numerical framework: (1) NLFFF magnetic extrapolation, (2) guiding-center simulations of energetic electron transport including magnetic mirroring and pitch-angle scattering, and (3) fully kinetic PIC simulations using evolved velocity distribution functions (EVDFs) sampled after ~one bounce period.
AI.result: The evolved beam distribution predominantly excites beam-Langmuir waves and fundamental plasma emission along the loop, with emission intensity decreasing from loop top toward footpoint. Harmonic emission is inefficient due to modest initial beam velocity. The model reproduces SPDB characteristics including ~4s duration and frequency drift behavior.
AI.conclusion: Plasma emission explains the mechanisms behind SPDB generation. The work demonstrates the feasibility of a unified model connecting coronal magnetic topology, particle transport, and radio emission.

title: Double-periodic pulsations simultaneously detected in mid-infrared and hard X-ray emissions during an X1.5 flare
comment: accepted for publication in A&A, comments are welcome
summary: Quasi-periodic pulsations (QPPs) have been observed in a broad electromagnetic spectrum, encompassing radio, ultraviolet, white light, X-rays, and Gama-rays. Yet, flare-associated QPPs remain rarely detected in mid-infrared emission. Here, we explored dual-period QPPs in the mid-infrared waveband, hard X-rays (HXRs), and microwave emission during an X1.5 flare on 2024 December 30 (SOL2024-12-30T04:01). Flare QPPs with dual quasi-periods at about 8.5 s and 4.6 s were simultaneously detected in the AIMS 8-10 um, Fermi 26-50 keV, and HXI 20-50 keV wavebands during the impulsive phase. Imaging observations show that the flare emission sources in the mid-infrared, HXR, and white-light wavebands are spatially coincident. Mid-infrared emissions are primarily localized at the loop top and double footpoints, which are connected by a hot plasma loop. These observational features support intermittent and rapid energy release via oscillatory magnetic reconnection during the solar flare. Differential emission measure analysis confirms fast sausage waves in flaring loops, while the WST observation indicates a white-light flare. We localized the flare QPPs with double periods in mid-infrared, HXR, and microwave emissions during a white-light flare. The flare-associated QPPs may be attributed to a quasi-periodic regime of magnetic reconnection, with the double periods likely modulated by quasi-harmonics of sausage waves.

AI.tldr: The paper reports detection of dual-period quasi-periodic pulsations (QPPs) at ~8.5 s and ~4.6 s in mid-infrared (AIMS 8-10 µm), hard X-rays (Fermi 26-50 keV, HXI 20-50 keV), and microwaves during an X1.5 white-light flare on 2024 December 30. The emissions are spatially co-located at loop tops and footpoints, supporting oscillatory magnetic reconnection as the mechanism, with double periods likely modulated by quasi-harmonics of sausage waves.
AI.motivation: Flare-associated QPPs are rarely detected in the mid-infrared waveband. This study aims to investigate the presence and characteristics of QPPs in mid-infrared emission alongside hard X-rays and microwaves during a solar flare.
AI.method: The authors analyzed multi-wavelength observations from AIMS (mid-infrared 8-10 µm), Fermi/GBM (26-50 keV), HXI (20-50 keV), and WST (white-light) during the impulsive phase of an X1.5 flare. They performed timing analysis to extract QPP periods, imaging to localize emission sources, and differential emission measure (DEM) analysis to diagnose wave modes.
AI.result: Dual quasi-periods at ~8.5 s and ~4.6 s were simultaneously detected in mid-infrared, HXR, and microwave emissions. Mid-infrared sources were co-located with HXR and white-light sources at loop tops and double footpoints. DEM analysis confirmed fast sausage waves. A white-light flare was observed by WST.
AI.conclusion: The dual-period QPPs in mid-infrared, HXR, and microwave emissions are attributed to a quasi-periodic regime of magnetic reconnection, with the double periods likely modulated by quasi-harmonics of sausage waves in flaring loops.

title: Ionized gas emission in protoplanetary disks with the SKAO
comment: Published in Advancing Astrophysics with the SKA II (AASKAII), 2026 (arXiv:2606.20366). Report-no:AASKAII/Guidi01. Advancing Astrophysics with the SKA II (AASKAII) outlines the transformative scientific advances that will be enabled by the SKA telescopes
summary: Protoplanetary disks represent a crucial stage in the evolution of Young Stellar Objects towards the formation of fully formed planetary systems. While substantial progress has been made in the last decades in the characterization of the dust and molecular gas in these systems, the ionized component remains poorly understood. Ionized gas traces important processes such as photoevaporation, accretion, disk winds, and jets, and therefore is key to studying disk dynamics, evolution, and ultimately planet formation. In this paper, we investigate the capabilities of the forthcoming SKA telescope to probe this component in protoplanetary disks within nearby star forming regions. We present state-of-the-art simulations of photoevaporative, magneto-thermal, and magnetohydrodynamic winds, and generate theoretical predictions and synthetic SKAO observations to assess its potential in detecting and characterizing free-free emission and Hydrogen recombination lines. Finally, we discuss synergies with complementary facilities and how they will provide a comprehensive, multi-scale view of disk winds and offer critical insights on the mechanisms driving disk evolution and the onset of planet formation.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the capabilities of the upcoming SKA telescope to study ionized gas in protoplanetary disks, using simulations of various disk winds to generate synthetic observations and assess detection potential for free-free emission and Hydrogen recombination lines.
AI.motivation: The ionized gas component in protoplanetary disks (tracing photoevaporation, accretion, disk winds, and jets) remains poorly understood despite progress in dust and molecular gas characterization, yet it is crucial for studying disk dynamics, evolution, and planet formation.
AI.method: The authors present state-of-the-art simulations of photoevaporative, magneto-thermal, and magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) winds, generate theoretical predictions and synthetic SKA Observatory observations to assess detection potential for free-free emission and Hydrogen recombination lines.
AI.result: The paper provides assessments of SKA's potential to detect and characterize free-free emission and Hydrogen recombination lines from ionized gas in protoplanetary disks within nearby star-forming regions.
AI.conclusion: SKA will provide a comprehensive, multi-scale view of disk winds through synergies with complementary facilities, offering critical insights on disk evolution mechanisms and the onset of planet formation.
